topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room hummed with an irritating monotony. I sat on the edge of the bench, my hands fiddling with the frayed edge of my jersey. Coach Miller stood by the whiteboard, his chalk dust a fine, grey film on his worn sneakers. He’d just delivered the news—a roster cut. I stared at the scuffed floor, my gaze fixed on a scuff mark near the water fountain. Every word he’d spoken seemed to have dissolved into static in my head. Why? I tried to process it, to find the logical thread connecting the last practice, the late-night strategy sessions, and this sudden, sharp dismissal. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, the movement feeling heavy, disconnected. When he finally turned, his expression was set, unyielding. "It's about fit, kid," he stated, his voice flat. I just nodded, the movement stiff, unable to form a coherent response.

[story 2]

My knuckles were white where I gripped my water bottle. The air in the gym felt thick, suffocating. When Coach announced the roster changes, a strange hollowness expanded in my chest. I watched his face, searching for any flicker of rationale, any clue that explained this sudden severing of connection. My jaw tightened involuntarily; I felt a hot flush creeping up my neck. I looked down at my cleats, the leather suddenly feeling alien beneath my fingers. I tried to speak, to ask what had happened, but the words caught in my throat, tasting like ash. He didn't elaborate, just pointed toward the door. The silence that followed was immense, pressing in on me until I felt a strange, dizzying lightness, as if the ground beneath my feet had momentarily shifted out from under me.

[story 3]

I stood by the exit, my backpack feeling impossibly heavy. The noise of the other players packing up seemed distant, muffled. Coach Miller had just finished speaking, his tone final, leaving no room for debate. I kept running a hand through my hair, a repetitive, anxious motion. My breathing hitched slightly, shallow and quick. I looked at the team huddled near the bench, their faces a mixture of confusion and disappointment. I tried to formulate a counter-argument, a defense of my performance, but the words simply wouldn't assemble themselves. It was like trying to catch smoke. A deep sense of bewilderment settled over me, a thick, unmovable feeling in my stomach that made me want to simply walk away from the entire scene.
